cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if the sentence "Luke Voit was out at first." is plausible. Let me start by breaking down what this means. First, "Luke Voit" is a name. I believe he is a professional baseball player. I'll check my knowledge to confirm: Luke Voit is a first baseman who has played for several Major League Baseball (MLB) teams, including the New York Yankees. That makes sense. Next, the phrase "was out at first." is a baseball term. In baseball, when a batter hits the ball and reaches base, but is later tagged out or forced out at first base before advancing to second base, they are said to have been "out at first." This can happen in various situations, such as: 1. A ground ball is hit to the infield, and the batter fails to reach first base in time before the throw from the fielder. 2. A fly ball is caught by an outfielder, and the batter fails to tag up and make it to first base (though this is less common - usually tag up is relevant for runners already on base, not the batter). 3. A bunt or sacrifice play where the batter intentionally gets out to allow a teammate to advance. So the sentence means that in a baseball game, Luke Voit batted and was put out at first base, meaning he didn't successfully reach first base and was out instead. Now, I need to assess if this is a plausible event. Since Luke Voit is a professional baseball player who plays as a first baseman, it's very plausible that he would bat and get out at first base. Being "out at first" is a common outcome in baseball, and it happens to all players, regardless of skill level. Even the best hitters get out more often than they get on base. It's also worth noting that the sentence is grammatically correct and uses standard baseball terminology. The verb "was out" is used in the passive voice, which is common in sports reporting. The phrase "at first" clearly refers to first base. Therefore, the sentence "Luke Voit was out at first." is plausible.
